worth_it_explanation: Pre-bid avoidance stops the bid before it reaches unsafe content, which means the impression is never purchased and no budget is spent on it. Post-bid reporting can identify placements after the fact, but the spend is already gone and the ad may have already appeared. The check is a settings review inside The Trade Desk UI, not a rebuild, and the gap between an ad group with avoidance and one without is visible in a few minutes of work.
intro: Pre-bid avoidance applies brand safety and suitability controls at the moment of the bid request, before any money changes hands. Integrations with DoubleVerify (DV) and Integral Ad Science (IAS) let The Trade Desk read a third-party classification of the impression opportunity and decline to bid on content that falls outside the brand's standards. The checks in this subcategory confirm that pre-bid avoidance is active, that the content category selections reflect actual brand standards rather than default settings, and that avoidance is applied consistently across all ad groups in a campaign rather than on a selective handful.

audit_checks.title: Pre-bid brand safety avoidance is not applied at the ad group level
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without pre-bid avoidance, the platform bids on impressions before any safety classification is applied. Budget reaches unsafe or unsuitable content, and any post-bid reporting only documents the damage after the spend is gone. For campaigns running at scale, the exposure accumulates quickly and the only remedy is a manual exclusion list built after the fact.

educate.base:
Pre-bid avoidance connects a DoubleVerify (DV) or Integral Ad Science (IAS) integration to an ad group so The Trade Desk can evaluate each impression opportunity against a brand safety classification before submitting a bid. If the content is classified as unsafe or unsuitable, no bid is placed and no money is spent. Post-bid reporting works differently: the impression is purchased and the ad may run before the classification is applied, which means the exposure already happened and the spend is already gone by the time a report flags it.

The Trade Desk surfaces this control at the ad group level under the 'Targeting' section, where a third-party verification provider can be selected and the avoidance settings configured. An ad group with no provider selected, or one where a provider is selected but avoidance is set to reporting-only, does not filter impressions before the bid.

A few signs point to pre-bid avoidance being absent or inactive:
  • The ad group's verification settings show no third-party provider selected
  • A provider is selected but the mode is set to 'reporting-only' rather than blocking or avoidance
  • Post-bid reports surface placements on content categories that the brand would not sanction if pre-bid filtering were on
You may want to confirm the provider is set to an avoidance mode rather than only a reporting mode. The distinction between the two options is easy to miss during initial setup.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, pre-bid avoidance on prospecting and retargeting campaigns limits brand exposure to content that conflicts with the purchase environment the brand wants to project. You may want to confirm that avoidance is applied on both upper-funnel prospecting ad groups and lower-funnel retargeting ad groups. The two types are sometimes configured separately.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, the quality of the environment where ads appear can influence the perceived credibility of the brand behind the form submission. You may want to confirm that avoidance is active on all ad groups driving form traffic, including any that use private marketplace (PMP) deals. PMP ad groups sometimes have their pre-bid settings configured independently.

learn_more.source_title: Brand Safety and Ad Fraud Standards

investigate.intro: Review each ad group in the campaign to confirm a verification provider is selected and that the avoidance mode is set to blocking rather than reporting-only.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign in The Trade Desk UI and navigate to the ad groups list.
For each ad group, open the 'Targeting' section and locate the third-party verification settings. Note which provider, if any, is selected (DoubleVerify or IAS).

For ad groups with a provider selected, confirm the mode. A setting of 'reporting-only' means the provider scores the impression but does not prevent the bid. Only a setting that actively blocks or avoids classified content filters before the bid.
Record any ad group that has no provider selected, or that has a provider in reporting-only mode, as a candidate for remediation.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each ad group, whether a verification provider is selected and whether the mode is avoidance or reporting-only. An ad group with a provider in avoidance mode passes. An ad group with no provider, or a provider in reporting-only mode, is a fail recorded with the ad group name and the current setting.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the verification provider setting and the mode for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that pre-bid brand safety avoidance is not applied on one or more ad groups in {{site_name}}'s campaigns on The Trade Desk. Without pre-bid filtering, the platform bids on impressions before any safety classification is applied, and budget reaches content that falls outside the brand's standards before any report can surface it. {{voice_recommend}} selecting a third-party verification provider on each affected ad group and confirming the mode is set to avoidance rather than reporting-only. After the change, review post-bid placement reports over a short window to confirm the filtered categories are no longer appearing.

audit_checks.title: Pre-bid avoidance categories reflect default settings rather than reviewed brand standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Default avoidance categories represent an industry-wide floor, not a brand's specific standards. A brand that sells children's products may need to block categories the default allows; a news publisher's advertising arm may need categories the default blocks. An unreviewed default leaves a gap that is invisible until a specific placement or campaign report surfaces it.

educate.base:
When a verification provider is first connected to an ad group in The Trade Desk, it applies a default set of blocked content categories. These defaults typically reflect an industry-consensus floor for content that is widely considered unsuitable, such as illegal activity or graphic violence. They do not reflect any individual brand's standards for the content it wants to appear alongside or the audiences it wants to reach.

Content categories in DV and IAS map to the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy, which organizes web content into a tiered hierarchy. Blocks can be set at broad category levels or at more specific subcategory levels, and brands can also add segments from the verification vendor's own suitability tiers. An unreviewed default leaves both over-blocking and under-blocking as possibilities: categories that the brand would accept remain blocked, reducing reach, and categories the brand would not sanction remain open.

A few signs point to avoidance categories not having been reviewed:
  • The provider settings show the defaults that were present at initial setup with no subsequent changes documented
  • The blocked categories do not match any brand safety guidelines the advertiser maintains
  • The account has no record of a category review since the provider was connected
You may want to ask the account team whether the current category selections were deliberately set or accepted as defaults. Default acceptance is the more common path.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser, the category review should include checking whether the brand's product categories create any content adjacency considerations. A brand selling alcohol, for example, may need to review the default treatment of content related to alcohol references. The default may block contexts that are actually appropriate for the brand.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, the category review should include the suitability tiers the provider offers beyond the floor categories. Content that passes the default floor may still be misaligned with the audience a lead generation campaign is trying to attract. You may want to confirm whether the brand's guidelines extend to suitability considerations beyond the baseline blocked categories.

investigate.intro: Review the current content category settings against any brand guidelines the advertiser maintains, and confirm whether the settings have been modified from the provider's defaults.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the ad group's verification settings and expand the content category avoidance section for the connected provider.

Record which category tiers are blocked. Note whether any categories appear to be the provider's out-of-the-box defaults or whether the list shows signs of deliberate customization, such as categories added or removed relative to a standard starting set.

Ask the account team for the brand's written brand safety guidelines, if they exist. Compare the blocked categories to the guidelines and record any categories that the guidelines would include but the avoidance settings do not block, and any that the settings block but the guidelines would allow.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the current category settings appear to be unmodified defaults and whether they have been compared to a written brand safety policy. Settings with documented review and alignment to brand guidelines pass. Settings that match the provider's defaults without any record of review, or settings that diverge from written brand guidelines in ways not deliberately chosen, are a fail recorded with the specific gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the current avoidance category settings and compare them to any available brand guidelines above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the pre-bid avoidance category settings on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk ad groups have not been reviewed against the brand's specific standards. The categories currently blocked reflect provider defaults rather than a deliberate configuration for this brand, which leaves the possibility that the brand appears alongside content it would not sanction or is excluded from content it would find suitable. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the current blocked category list to the brand's written safety guidelines and adjusting the settings to reflect actual brand standards. After the review, documenting the approved configuration makes future audits faster and provides a baseline to detect drift.

audit_checks.title: Pre-bid avoidance is applied on some ad groups but missing on others within the same campaign
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An ad group without pre-bid avoidance operates as if the protection does not exist for the campaign. Budget flowing through the unprotected ad group reaches the same unsafe content the other ad groups were configured to avoid, and spend distribution naturally shifts toward the unprotected ad groups over time because they face less filtering and can bid on a broader set of impressions.

educate.base:
Pre-bid avoidance in The Trade Desk is configured at the ad group level, not at the campaign level. A campaign can have some ad groups with avoidance set and others without, and the platform does not flag the inconsistency. The result is that the campaign's brand safety posture is only as strong as its weakest ad group.

The gap appears most often when new ad groups are added to a campaign after the original setup. The existing ad groups carry forward their avoidance settings; newly created ad groups start without a provider unless one is explicitly added. It also appears when ad groups are duplicated or when a template is used that does not include the avoidance configuration.

The Trade Desk's automated bidding (Koa) will allocate spend toward ad groups that can access more inventory. An ad group without pre-bid avoidance faces less filtering and can bid on a wider set of impressions, so it naturally receives more spend over time. This means the gap is not just theoretical: the unprotected ad group draws budget toward it.

A few signs point to inconsistent avoidance across ad groups:
  • Some ad groups show a verification provider in the targeting settings while others show none
  • Ad groups added after the original campaign launch have different provider settings than the original ad groups
  • Spend concentration in reporting shows ad groups without provider settings receiving a disproportionate share of budget
You may want to review all ad groups, not just the ones that are currently spending the most. A low-spend ad group can still have the avoidance gap.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For an ecommerce advertiser running multiple ad groups targeting different product categories or audience segments, each ad group should carry the same avoidance baseline. You may want to confirm that ad groups for high-margin product lines or seasonal campaigns created after the original launch have the same configuration as the original ad groups.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead generation advertiser, inconsistent avoidance often appears between ad groups using open exchange inventory and those using curated private marketplace (PMP) deals. You may want to confirm that both types carry the pre-bid avoidance configuration. PMP ad groups are sometimes excluded from the same targeting template used for open exchange ad groups.

learn_more.source_title: Brand Safety and Ad Fraud Standards

investigate.intro: Review every ad group in the campaign and record the verification provider settings for each, then identify any ad groups where the configuration differs from the majority.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign and switch to the ad groups list view.
Open each ad group's 'Targeting' settings and note the verification provider status. Record whether a provider is selected, which provider it is, and whether the mode is avoidance or reporting-only.
After reviewing all ad groups, identify any where the provider setting differs from the others. Note the ad group name and the gap.
Check the creation dates of the ad groups that are missing avoidance to determine whether they were added after the original campaign setup, which is a common cause of the gap.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the verification provider setting for every ad group in the campaign. If all ad groups have the same provider in avoidance mode, the check passes. If one or more ad groups have no provider, or have a different provider or mode than the others, those ad groups are a fail recorded by name with the specific discrepancy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the verification provider setting for every ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that pre-bid avoidance is applied inconsistently across {{site_name}}'s ad groups on The Trade Desk. Some ad groups carry a verification provider in avoidance mode while others do not, which means the campaign's brand safety coverage has gaps. The Trade Desk's automated bidding favors ad groups that can access more inventory, so ad groups without avoidance attract a larger share of spend over time. {{voice_recommend}} adding the same verification provider and avoidance settings to each ad group that is missing them, with priority on the highest-spending ad groups. After the update, a quick review of all ad groups confirms the settings are consistent before the campaign continues.
